name: Cement Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Cement. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for cement surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for cement absorption and minimal substrate damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for effective cleaning resolution

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal from cement surfaces

pulseWidth.description: Short pulse duration for efficient contaminant removal with minimal heat diffusion

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning efficiency

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage

power.description: Typical range for industrial laser cleaning of cementitious materials like concrete to remove contaminants without thermal damage; starting at 100W balances efficiency and safety. Max safe below 500W to avoid cracking porous substrate. Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics YLR series for stone/concrete cleaning; academic paper by Marczak et al. (2012) on laser cleaning of historical concrete.

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for 1064 nm cleaning of cement; slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy contamination like oil/graffiti, faster (2000-3000 mm/s) for light dust. Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal removal with minimal passes. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe guidelines; case studies in Journal of Cultural Heritage (2015) on concrete surface preparation.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cement due to cost-effectiveness and sufficient ablation; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond reduces heat-affected zone (HAZ) but unnecessary for porous, low-conductivity cement. Recommended 20 ns minimizes thermal damage. Source: Laser physics research in Applied Surface Science (2018) by Anisimov et al. on pulse duration effects for dielectrics like cement.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed cleaning to control heat accumulation in low-thermal-conductivity cement; higher rates (>50 kHz) speed up process but risk overheating/cracking. Recommended 30 kHz balances speed and safety. Source: Academic literature in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2020) by Pou et al. on repetition rate trade-offs for stone materials.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant ablation on cement without substrate damage; damage threshold ~4 J/cm² for Portland cement due to high reflectivity (~30% at 1064 nm) and low melting point (~1200°C). Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for safe removal of surface layers. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2016) by Colombier et al. on concrete-like materials.

spot_size.description: Beam diameter for uniform cleaning; smaller (100-200 μm) for fine surface prep, larger (300-500 μm) for broad areas. Recommended 200 μm for cement to achieve good resolution on rough surfaces. Source: Equipment specifications from Adapt Laser Systems scanners for industrial cleaning; process studies in Lasers in Manufacturing (2019).

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers on cement to avoid excessive energy input. Recommended 2 passes for complete removal with uniform results. Source: Industry practice from case studies in Construction and Building Materials (2017) on laser-prepared cement surfaces.

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage on irregular cement surfaces; 30-50% for large areas, 60-80% for detailed cleaning. Recommended 50% prevents gaps while minimizing overexposure. Source: Process optimization studies in Optics Express (2014) by Lorenz et al. on scan overlap for ablation uniformity.
